howItWorks.intro: RV propane consumption depends on which appliances are running and for how long. Each appliance has a BTU rating that determines its fuel draw.
rules: Each appliance consumes propane based on its BTU/hour rating
Daily gallons = total BTU/hour × hours / 91,500 BTU per gallon
howItWorks.outro: The furnace is typically the biggest propane consumer in cold weather. Running on electric hookups when available saves significant propane. Consider a catalytic heater as a more efficient alternative to the furnace.

faq.question: How long does a 20 lb propane tank last in an RV?
faq.answer: A 20 lb tank holds 4.7 gallons of propane. With moderate usage (furnace 8 hrs, fridge 24 hrs, stove 1 hr, water heater 2 hrs), it lasts about 3-5 days. In warm weather with just fridge and cooking, it can last 2-3 weeks. In cold weather with heavy furnace use, it might only last 2 days.

faq.question: How long does a 30 lb propane tank last in an RV?
faq.answer: A 30 lb tank holds 7.1 gallons. With moderate usage, expect 5-8 days. With light usage (no furnace), it can last 3-4 weeks. Many RVs have two 30 lb tanks for a combined 14.2 gallons, which doubles these estimates.

faq.question: What uses the most propane in an RV?
faq.answer: The furnace is by far the biggest consumer at 30,000-40,000 BTU/hr. Running a furnace for 8 hours uses as much propane as running a fridge for nearly a week. The water heater is the second biggest consumer. The fridge in propane mode is very efficient at only 1,500 BTU/hr.

faq.question: How many BTU per gallon of propane?
faq.answer: One gallon of propane contains 91,500 BTU of energy. One pound of propane contains about 21,600 BTU. A 20 lb tank holds about 4.7 gallons (429,450 BTU total), and a 30 lb tank holds about 7.1 gallons (649,650 BTU total).

faq.question: How can I reduce propane usage in my RV?
faq.answer: Use electric hookups when available for fridge and water heater. Use a portable electric space heater instead of the furnace. Cook with an electric cooktop or outdoor grill. Insulate windows and doors. Use a tank blanket in cold weather. A catalytic heater uses 50-75% less propane than a forced air furnace.

faq.question: Should I run my RV fridge on propane or electric?
faq.answer: Use electric when you have hookups or a generator running — it's essentially free and works better in hot weather. Use propane when boondocking. The propane fridge uses only 1,500 BTU/hr (about 0.4 gallons per day), so it's very efficient and won't drain your tanks quickly.
